title: ADK-304: High-Throughput Message Queues for Agents
description: Scale A2A traffic with asynchronous message brokers like RabbitMQ or Kafka, implementing retry policies and DLQs.

highlights.title: Asynchronous Brokers
highlights.description: Offload agent messages to RabbitMQ, decoupling synchronous execution threads.

highlights.title: Retry Policies
highlights.description: Design delivery retry protocols and exponential backoff loops for failed agent calls.

highlights.title: Dead Letter Queues
highlights.description: Redirect unresolvable agent transaction payloads to DLQs for developer audit analysis.

modules.title: 1. The necessity of asynchronous execution in multi-agent routing

blocks.content: Asynchronous Execution Necessity

Executing multi-agent tasks synchronously blocks connection threads. If Agent A calls Agent B, which calls Agent C, any network jitter or model delay will cascade, leading to timeout errors. Asynchronous queues solve this.
Sync vs Async Call Graph

blocks.title: Message Queue Paradigm
blocks.content: In an async queue, Agent A writes a message to the broker and returns a taskId. Workers pick up the task and update the taskId state, decoupling the pipeline.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What problem does asynchronous queueing solve in agent communications?
- A) It increases model temperatures.
- B) It prevents cascading timeouts by decoupling agent call requests from task completion.
- C) It compiles python bindings.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Decoupling stops network timeouts.
RabbitMQ Integration with A2A
A2AServer supports RabbitMQ out of the box. Instead of using standard local memory routing, we configure the daemon to listen to an AMQP broker.
Python AMQP Listener Setup

blocks.code:
import pika

connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters('localhost'))
channel = connection.channel()

# Declare queue for agent communications
channel.queue_declare(queue='agent_tasks')
print("Connected to RabbitMQ task broker.")

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: Which protocol does RabbitMQ utilize for message transportation?
- A) HTTP
- B) AMQP
- C) FTP

Feedback: Option B is correct. AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) is the standard for RabbitMQ.

modules.title: 3. Designing worker agents that consume queues

blocks.content: Worker Agent Consumers
Worker agents run in background processes, listening to queues. When a task arrives, they run the agent, process the query, and publish results to an output queue.
Worker Consumer Code

blocks.code:
def on_request(ch, method, props, body):
    query = body.decode()
    print(f"Processing task: {query}")
    # Run agent logic
    result = "Processed: " + query
    
    # Publish back response
    ch.basic_publish(exchange='', routing_key=props.reply_to, body=result)
    ch.basic_ack(delivery_tag=method.delivery_tag)

blocks.title: Acknowledge Messages
blocks.content: Always send an acknowledgment (basic_ack) after processing finishes. If the worker crashes, the broker will re-queue the task.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What happens if a consumer worker crashes before acknowledging a task?
- A) The message is deleted by the broker.
- B) The broker re-queues the message so another worker can process it.
- C) The queue crashes.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Unacknowledged messages are re-queued to maintain reliability.

modules.title: 4. Implementing retry policies, transaction rollbacks, and DLQs

blocks.content: Retries & Dead Letter Queues

If a worker crashes 5 times on the same message (e.g. malformed inputs causing parser errors), it should not loop forever. You must limit retries and route the message to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for audit.

items.title: Retry Limit
items.content: Attempt a failed message a maximum of 3 times with exponential delays.

items.title: DLQ Route
items.content: Redirect messages exceeding retry counts to a 'dlq_queue' channel.

items.title: Audit Trail
items.content: Alert developers of DLQ entries for debugging.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What is the primary role of a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)?
- A) To accelerate execution speed.
- B) To store messages that failed to process successfully after multiple retry attempts.
- C) To handle user passwords.

Feedback: Option B is correct. DLQs store persistently failing tasks.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Building an asynchronous order-processing agent queue

blocks.content: Lab: Building an Asynchronous Order-Processing Agent Queue
In this lab, you will write a complete Python script simulating a message queue dispatcher that puts order tasks in a local list queue and processes them asynchronously.
Step 1: Write the queue processor

blocks.code:
import queue
import time

# Mocking an AMQP queue
task_queue = queue.Queue()

def produce_task(order_id, details):
    task = {"id": order_id, "details": details}
    task_queue.put(task)
    print(f"Enqueued Order: {order_id}")

def consume_tasks():
    while not task_queue.empty():
        task = task_queue.get()
        print(f"Processing Order {task['id']}: {task['details']}")
        time.sleep(0.1) # Simulate tool latency
        task_queue.task_done()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    produce_task(1001, "Item: Laptop")
    produce_task(1002, "Item: Monitor")
    consume_tasks()

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What does task_queue.task_done() signal?
- A) That the broker must be restarted.
- B) That the enqueued task was successfully processed.
- C) That the queue is empty.

Feedback: Option B is correct. It signals task completion to the queue manager.
